nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sandy or mixed sand, rubble, and coral areas of reef flats and lagoons; depth range 20–68 m

diet.notes: Has been documented eating live copepods, amphipods, and vitamin-enriched live baby brine shrimp; some individuals have been reported to transition over time to frozen Cyclops, small Mysis, and brine shrimp. Generally advised to target-feed multiple times daily. Readily consumes red bugs (Tegastes acroporanus micro-amphipods) from Acropora corals, which has made it of interest to reef keepers dealing with red bug infestations.

summary: The Dragonface Pipefish is a challenging expert-only species from the Western Pacific, generally considered reef-safe and noted for consuming red bugs on Acropora. It tends to require live foods and a calm, mature reef with established copepod populations.
careNotes: The Dragonface Pipefish (Corythoichthys intestinalis) is generally advised for expert-level aquarists due to demanding feeding requirements. Rather than swimming actively through the water column, this species tends to move by sliding along the substrate and through rockwork, so it is easily outcompeted and stressed by aggressive or fast-moving tankmates. Species-only or pipefish-and-seahorse setups are strongly recommended to ensure the pipefish can feed without competition. A mature reef with established copepod and amphipod populations is generally considered the most suitable environment; these naturally occurring prey animals are the foundation of its diet. Filtration intakes should be shielded with strainers to prevent injury, as pipefish can be drawn in by suction. Males carry developing young in a brood patch, and breeding has been reported in well-maintained aquariums.
breedingNotes: Males carry eggs in a brood pouch on the ventral surface. Pairs have been reported to breed in captivity when well-fed and kept in low-stress environments. Breeding difficulty is generally considered advanced due to the challenges of feeding and sustaining adults, let alone raising fry.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range, adult size (17.8 cm max), and temperature range (25.3–28.7°C). FishBase lists higher temperature range than hobby sources; averaged across all four sources.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size ('almost 5 inches'), tank minimum (30 gal), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.020–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4). Adult size smaller than FishBase; averaged into adultSizeCm range.

additional.name: AquamarinePower Pipefish Care Guide
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: temperature 74–78°F, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, tank 30–50 gal, lifespan 5–10 years. Used for averaging temperature minimum and tank volume.

fish.breedingNotes: Males are ovoviviparous and carry developing young in a ventral brood patch. Breeding has been reported in aquariums when adults are well-fed and unstressed; raising fry successfully is considered very challenging due to their tiny size and need for live microfoods.
